================================================================================
                    CREWAI — AGENTS & SKILLS IN DETAIL
================================================================================
  Based on the CREW/ workspace at /home/me/Notebooks/CREW
  Framework: CrewAI (Python 3.11)
  Key source: src/crewai_skill_demo/tools/world_lookup_tool.py
================================================================================


────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 1 — CONCEPTUAL OVERVIEW
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

What is CrewAI?
───────────────
CrewAI is a Python framework for orchestrating multiple AI agents that
collaborate to complete complex tasks. Think of it like a crew of specialists
aboard a ship — each member has a distinct role, goal, and a set of tools
(skills) they know how to use. CrewAI wires those crew members together with
tasks and a shared mission.

The three core building blocks are:
  1. Skills  — what an agent CAN DO  (implemented as Tools / BaseTool subclasses)
  2. Agents  — who does the work     (role, goal, backstory, tools)
  3. Crew    — how they work together (tasks, process, agents)

This document focuses deeply on Skills and Agents.


────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 2 — SKILLS  (CrewAI Tools / BaseTool)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

What is a "Skill"?
──────────────────
In the current CrewAI documentation, a reusable agent "skill" is implemented
as a custom Tool — specifically a subclass of `crewai.tools.BaseTool`.
The terms "skill" and "tool" are interchangeable in this context.

A skill gives an agent a concrete capability it can call at any time during its
reasoning loop: look up data, call an API, parse a file, run a calculation, etc.
The LLM running the agent decides when to invoke a skill and with what input;
the skill executes deterministically and returns a result string.

The BaseTool Contract
─────────────────────
Every skill must satisfy three required class attributes and one method:

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  name:        str              — machine-readable identifier               │
  │  description: str              — natural-language description for the LLM  │
  │  args_schema: Type[BaseModel]  — Pydantic model defining valid inputs       │
  │  _run(self, **kwargs) -> str   — the actual implementation                 │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Why each matters:

  name
    The LLM selects which tool to call by this identifier. It should be a
    concise snake_case string, e.g. "traveller_world_lookup". Names must be
    unique within a crew.

  description
    This is the primary signal the LLM uses to decide whether to call the tool.
    Write it like a one-sentence docstring: start with a verb, be specific.
    A vague description leads to the agent calling the wrong tool or not calling
    it at all.

  args_schema
    A Pydantic v2 BaseModel class. Each field on the model becomes a parameter
    the LLM must supply when it invokes the tool. The framework validates the
    LLM's output against this schema before calling _run(), preventing malformed
    inputs. Always use `Field(..., description="...")` so the LLM knows what
    each field means.

  _run(self, ...)
    The real Python code that executes the skill. It receives the validated
    field values from args_schema as keyword arguments. It must return a str —
    that string is handed back to the agent as the "tool result" and gets
    included in the agent's reasoning context.


The Full Pattern (Minimal)
──────────────────────────
  from typing import Type
  from crewai.tools import BaseTool
  from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

  class MySkillInput(BaseModel):
      value: str = Field(..., description="The input string for the skill.")

  class MySkillTool(BaseTool):
      name: str = "my_skill"
      description: str = "Processes a value and returns a transformed result."
      args_schema: Type[BaseModel] = MySkillInput

      def _run(self, value: str) -> str:
          return f"Handled: {value}"

This is the exact pattern documented in CREW/README.md (lines 56–73).


Real Example: WorldLookupTool
──────────────────────────────
File: src/crewai_skill_demo/tools/world_lookup_tool.py

  from typing import Type
  from crewai.tools import BaseTool
  from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


  class WorldLookupInput(BaseModel):
      """Validated input schema for the world lookup skill."""
      world_name: str = Field(..., description="Traveller world name to look up.")


  class WorldLookupTool(BaseTool):
      """Example CrewAI tool backed by a small in-memory Traveller catalogue."""

      name: str = "traveller_world_lookup"
      description: str = "Looks up a small local Traveller world catalogue by name."
      args_schema: Type[BaseModel] = WorldLookupInput

      _WORLD_DATA: dict[str, str] = {
          "regina":   "Regina (1910): subsector capital, class A starport, major trade role.",
          "efate":    "Efate (1705): high-population world, strong Imperial center.",
          "rhylanor": "Rhylanor (2716): major Spinward Marches hub, strong commerce.",
      }

      def _run(self, world_name: str) -> str:
          key = world_name.strip().lower()          # normalise casing
          if key in self._WORLD_DATA:
              return self._WORLD_DATA[key]
          return (
              f"No local entry for '{world_name}'. "
              "Add it to WorldLookupTool._WORLD_DATA to expand this skill."
          )

Walkthrough of the real example:

  WorldLookupInput (the schema)
    • Declares one required field: world_name (str).
    • The Field(...) first argument is "..." which means required (no default).
    • The description is what the agent's LLM sees when deciding what to pass.

  WorldLookupTool (the skill)
    • name = "traveller_world_lookup" — short and descriptive.
    • description tells the LLM exactly what the tool does.
    • args_schema = WorldLookupInput — wires up validation.
    • _WORLD_DATA is a class-level dict — acts as the in-memory "database".
      Because this is a class variable (not an instance variable), it is shared
      across all instances, which is fine for read-only reference data.
    • _run() lower-cases the input to make lookups case-insensitive, then
      returns either the found record or a helpful "not found" message.

  Why store data in the tool?
    The demo is intentionally self-contained so it runs without external
    services, APIs, or database connections. In production you would replace
    _WORLD_DATA with a real DB query, API call, or file read.


How Skills Are Discovered and Invoked at Runtime
──────────────────────────────────────────────────
When an agent is running a task, CrewAI builds a prompt for the underlying
LLM that includes:

  1. The agent's role, goal, and backstory.
  2. The task description.
  3. A formatted list of available tools: for each tool, the name, description,
     and the JSON schema derived from args_schema.

The LLM reads this and decides: "I should call traveller_world_lookup with
world_name = 'Regina'."

CrewAI intercepts that decision, validates "Regina" against WorldLookupInput,
calls tool._run(world_name="Regina"), and injects the returned string back into
the LLM's context. The agent can then use that result to form its final answer.

This loop (reason → act → observe) is the ReAct pattern and is central to how
agentic systems work.


Exporting Skills (tools/__init__.py)
─────────────────────────────────────
File: src/crewai_skill_demo/tools/__init__.py

  from crewai_skill_demo.tools.world_lookup_tool import WorldLookupTool
  __all__ = ["WorldLookupTool"]

Every new tool file must be imported here and added to __all__. This lets
consumers do:

  from crewai_skill_demo.tools import WorldLookupTool

instead of importing the full dotted path. When you add a new skill, always
update this file.


Scaffolding a New Skill with the CLI
──────────────────────────────────────
Once crewai is installed you can auto-generate a tool scaffold:

  pipenv run crewai create tool my_skill_name

This creates a new file with the BaseTool boilerplate already filled in.
You then fill in the business logic in _run().


Testing Skills in Isolation
─────────────────────────────
Because _run() is a plain Python method, you can test skills without an LLM:

  tool = WorldLookupTool()
  result = tool._run("Regina")
  assert "Regina (1910)" in result

This is exactly what demo.py does:

  File: src/crewai_skill_demo/demo.py

    from crewai_skill_demo.tools.world_lookup_tool import WorldLookupTool

    def main() -> None:
        tool = WorldLookupTool()
        print(tool._run("Regina"))

    if __name__ == "__main__":
        main()

Run it with:
  PYTHONPATH=src pipenv run python -m crewai_skill_demo.demo

Expected output:
  Regina (1910): subsector capital with a class A starport and a major trade role.

And the smoke tests in tests/test_skill_demo.py verify this automatically:

  class SkillDemoSmokeTests(unittest.TestCase):
      def test_root_starter_demo_outputs_regina_lookup(self) -> None:
          output = self._run_demo(ROOT_STARTER)
          self.assertIn("Regina (1910)", output)

Run all tests:
  PYTHONPATH=src python -m pytest tests/


Skill Design Best Practices
─────────────────────────────
  1. One responsibility per skill.
     A skill should do one thing well. Don't build a "do everything" tool.
     The LLM has a better chance of calling the right tool when each has a
     narrow, clear purpose.

  2. Make descriptions LLM-friendly.
     Write the description as if you are telling a capable but literal
     assistant what the tool does. Include what inputs it expects and what
     format the output is in.

  3. Always validate with Pydantic.
     Never skip args_schema. LLMs can hallucinate parameters. Pydantic catches
     type mismatches and missing fields before they reach your code.

  4. Return plain strings.
     _run() must return str. If your data is structured (dict, list, object),
     convert it: return json.dumps(result) or return str(result).

  5. Handle errors gracefully.
     Return an informative error string rather than raising an exception.
     The agent can then decide what to do with that information.
     Example:
       if not found:
           return f"No entry for '{name}'. Try a different spelling."

  6. Keep skills runnable without credentials in development.
     Use local data (like _WORLD_DATA) for the demo path. Add API calls and
     environment variable reads behind conditionals so tests don't fail without
     secrets configured.

  7. Export from __init__.py.
     Always add new tools to tools/__init__.py so imports stay clean and
     consistent across the codebase.


────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 3 — AGENTS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

What is an Agent?
──────────────────
An Agent is an autonomous AI entity that has a role, a goal, a backstory, and
a set of tools (skills). It uses an LLM to reason about a task, decide which
tools to call and in what order, interpret the results, and ultimately produce
an output.

An agent is not a script. It does not follow a fixed sequence of steps.
It reasons dynamically — given its goal and the available tools, it figures out
a plan and executes it iteratively until it has a satisfactory answer.

Defining an Agent
──────────────────
  from crewai import Agent
  from crewai_skill_demo.tools.world_lookup_tool import WorldLookupTool

  researcher = Agent(
      role      = "Traveller researcher",
      goal      = "Answer world questions accurately",
      backstory = "Knows how to use a local world lookup skill.",
      tools     = [WorldLookupTool()],
      verbose   = True,
  )

This is the canonical example from CREW/README.md (lines 101–109).

Agent Parameters Explained
───────────────────────────

  role (str, required)
    The agent's professional title or function. Used in prompts to set context.
    Think of it as the job description. Example: "Senior Data Analyst",
    "Travel Researcher", "Code Reviewer".

  goal (str, required)
    The single driving objective this agent is trying to achieve. It should be
    specific and outcome-oriented. The agent uses this to evaluate whether its
    work is "done enough". Example: "Find and summarise recent news about the
    Spinward Marches subsectors."

  backstory (str, required)
    A prose paragraph (or sentence) describing who this agent is, what
    experience it has, and how it approaches problems. This is injected into the
    LLM system prompt to shape tone, reasoning style, and personality.
    Example: "A seasoned Traveller scout who has mapped dozens of subsectors
    and relies on data-driven research before drawing conclusions."

  tools (list[BaseTool], default=[])
    A list of instantiated BaseTool objects (skills) the agent is allowed to
    use. The agent sees the name and description of each tool and decides when
    to invoke them. An agent with no tools can only reason using its LLM
    knowledge — it cannot take external actions.

  verbose (bool, default=False)
    When True, CrewAI prints each reasoning step, tool call, and tool result
    to stdout. Essential during development so you can see what the agent is
    actually doing. Disable in production to reduce noise.

  llm (optional)
    The language model to use for this agent. If not specified, CrewAI uses
    the model configured in the environment (MODEL= in .env). Supports any
    LiteLLM-compatible model string, e.g. "openai/gpt-4.1-mini".

  memory (bool, default=False)
    When True, the agent remembers context across multiple task runs within
    a crew execution, allowing later tasks to build on earlier results.

  allow_delegation (bool, default=True)
    Allows this agent to hand off a subtask to another agent in the crew.
    Useful in multi-agent setups where agents specialise.

  max_iter (int, default=15)
    Maximum number of reasoning iterations before the agent is forced to stop
    and return whatever answer it has. Prevents infinite loops.

  step_callback (callable, optional)
    A function called after each agent step, useful for logging or monitoring.


The Agent Reasoning Loop (ReAct)
──────────────────────────────────
CrewAI agents follow the ReAct (Reason + Act) pattern:

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                                      │
  │  Thought: "I need to look up Regina. I'll use traveller_world_lookup"│
  │      ↓                                                               │
  │  Action: traveller_world_lookup(world_name="Regina")                 │
  │      ↓                                                               │
  │  Observation: "Regina (1910): subsector capital, class A starport…"  │
  │      ↓                                                               │
  │  Thought: "I have the data. I can now answer the task."              │
  │      ↓                                                               │
  │  Final Answer: "Regina is the subsector capital at hex 1910…"        │
  │                                                                      │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Each iteration consists of:
  1. Thought   — the LLM reasons about what to do next
  2. Action    — choose a tool and provide arguments
  3. Observation — the tool's return value is fed back to the LLM
  4. Repeat until the LLM produces a "Final Answer"

The agent's backstory, role, and goal all influence the Thought steps.
The tools available influence which Actions are possible.


Attaching Skills to Agents
───────────────────────────
Skills are attached at instantiation time via the tools= list:

  tools=[WorldLookupTool()]    # instantiated, not the class itself

Each tool in the list is available to the agent. The agent's LLM sees:

  Available Tools:
  - traveller_world_lookup: Looks up a small local Traveller world catalogue by name.
    Arguments: {"world_name": {"type": "string", "description": "Traveller world name to look up."}}

The richer and more accurate your tool's description and schema, the better
the agent can decide when and how to use it.

You can give an agent multiple skills:

  navigator = Agent(
      role="Navigator",
      goal="Plan safe jump routes through the Spinward Marches",
      backstory="Expert in astrogation with access to world and route data.",
      tools=[
          WorldLookupTool(),
          RoutePlannerTool(),
          FuelCalculatorTool(),
      ],
  )


Agents Without Tools
─────────────────────
An agent can have no tools. In that case it acts as a "pure reasoning" agent —
it uses only LLM knowledge and the context provided by other agents in the crew.

  analyst = Agent(
      role="Data Analyst",
      goal="Synthesise research into a clear summary",
      backstory="Excellent at drawing conclusions from gathered data.",
      tools=[],     # no tools — this agent only reasons
  )

In a multi-agent crew, a researcher agent (with tools) gathers data and a
synthesis agent (no tools) analyses and writes the final output.


The LLM Behind the Agent
─────────────────────────
Agents need an LLM to do their reasoning. This is configured via environment
variables in the .env file:

  OPENAI_API_KEY=replace-me
  MODEL=openai/gpt-4.1-mini

CrewAI uses LiteLLM under the hood, which means you can swap to any supported
provider by changing the MODEL= value:

  openai/gpt-4.1-mini     → OpenAI GPT-4.1 mini
  openai/gpt-4o           → OpenAI GPT-4o
  anthropic/claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022  → Anthropic Claude
  ollama/llama3           → Local Ollama model

The demo (demo.py) does NOT need an LLM because it calls tool._run() directly.
The LLM is only required when running a full crew where the agent must reason
about which tool to call.


────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 4 — FROM SKILL TO CREW: THE FULL PICTURE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Here is how everything fits together in a complete (minimal) crew:

  from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, Process
  from crewai_skill_demo.tools.world_lookup_tool import WorldLookupTool

  # 1. Define the skill
  lookup_skill = WorldLookupTool()

  # 2. Define the agent with that skill
  researcher = Agent(
      role="Traveller researcher",
      goal="Answer world questions accurately",
      backstory="Knows how to use a local world lookup skill.",
      tools=[lookup_skill],
      verbose=True,
  )

  # 3. Define a task for that agent
  task = Task(
      description="Look up the world Regina and summarise its key features.",
      expected_output="A short paragraph about Regina.",
      agent=researcher,
  )

  # 4. Assemble the crew
  crew = Crew(
      agents=[researcher],
      tasks=[task],
      process=Process.sequential,    # tasks run one after another
      verbose=True,
  )

  # 5. Run the crew
  result = crew.kickoff()
  print(result)

Data flow:
  crew.kickoff()
      → Task is given to researcher agent
      → Agent LLM reasons: "I should call traveller_world_lookup with Regina"
      → CrewAI validates "Regina" against WorldLookupInput
      → WorldLookupTool._run(world_name="Regina") executes
      → Returns "Regina (1910): subsector capital…"
      → LLM incorporates result into final answer
      → Task result returned to crew
      → crew.kickoff() returns final output


────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 5 — WORKSPACE FILE STRUCTURE (ANNOTATED)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

CREW/
├── .env.example                ← Copy to .env, add OPENAI_API_KEY + MODEL
│                                  Only needed for real LLM-driven crew runs
│
├── README.md                   ← Step-by-step guide; skill pattern; run commands
│
├── crew.ipynb                  ← Jupyter notebook with Traveller world data
│                                  experiments (UWP parsing, crew roster concepts)
│
├── src/
│   └── crewai_skill_demo/
│       ├── __init__.py         ← Package marker; docstring only
│       │
│       ├── demo.py             ← Entry point: instantiates WorldLookupTool
│       │                          and calls _run("Regina") directly
│       │                          Run: PYTHONPATH=src python -m crewai_skill_demo.demo
│       │
│       └── tools/
│           ├── __init__.py     ← Re-exports WorldLookupTool; add new tools here
│           └── world_lookup_tool.py  ← THE skill implementation
│                                        WorldLookupInput (schema)
│                                        WorldLookupTool (BaseTool subclass)
│
├── tests/
│   └── test_skill_demo.py      ← Smoke tests; both root and Crew/ copies tested
│                                  Run: PYTHONPATH=src python -m pytest tests/
│
└── Crew/                       ← Nested copy of the same starter
    ├── Pipfile                 ← crewai = "*", python_version = "3.11"
    ├── .env.example
    ├── README.md
    ├── src/  (identical layout)
    └── tests/ (single-copy smoke test)


────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 6 — QUICK REFERENCE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

SKILL CHECKLIST (for every new tool):
  ☐  Create new file in src/crewai_skill_demo/tools/my_tool.py
  ☐  Define Pydantic input model (MyToolInput) with Field(..., description=...)
  ☐  Define BaseTool subclass with name, description, args_schema
  ☐  Implement _run(self, ...) → str
  ☐  Add to tools/__init__.py and __all__
  ☐  Test in isolation: tool = MyTool(); print(tool._run(...))

AGENT CHECKLIST:
  ☐  Give a specific role, goal, and backstory
  ☐  Attach instantiated tool objects: tools=[MyTool()]
  ☐  Set verbose=True during development
  ☐  Configure LLM via .env (copy .env.example → .env)

COMMANDS:
  Install:    cd CREW && pipenv install --skip-lock
  Demo:       PYTHONPATH=src pipenv run python -m crewai_skill_demo.demo
  Tests:      PYTHONPATH=src python -m pytest tests/
  Scaffold:   pipenv run crewai create tool my_tool_name

KEY IMPORTS:
  from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, Process
  from crewai.tools import BaseTool
  from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
  from typing import Type

ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES (.env):
  OPENAI_API_KEY   — API key for the LLM provider
  MODEL            — LiteLLM model string, e.g. openai/gpt-4.1-mini

================================================================================
END OF DOCUMENT
